The Islands Architecture Is a Confession: If You Need Partial Hydration, You Already LostIslands architecture gets discussed in the frontend world as an innovation. A clever pattern. A new tool in the toolbox. I want to make a different argument: it’s an admission.
When an engineering community spends years building elaborate machinery to hydrate only part of a page instead of the whole thing, that machinery isn’t solving a new problem. It’s patching an old one, the one created by the decision to make full client-side hydration the default in the first place.
What Partial Hydration Actually Admits
The term “islands architecture” was popularized by Jason Miller, creator of Preact, in writing and talks that treated interactivity as the exception on a page rather than the rule. Under this model, most of a page is static HTML, and only specific components, the “islands”, get hydrated with JavaScript and become interactive. Everything else stays inert markup the browser never needs to wire up.
Sit with what that framing implies. If the natural, correct state of most content on most pages is static, and only small islands genuinely need client-side JavaScript, then the entire premise of shipping a full SPA runtime to hydrate the whole page was wrong from the start. Islands architecture isn’t proposing a new capability. It’s proposing a correction: stop doing the expensive thing everywhere, and only do it where it’s actually needed.
That’s not an enhancement to the SPA model. That’s an architecture built specifically to undo the SPA model’s default behavior.
The People Building These Systems Say So Directly
This isn’t an outside critique being projected onto framework authors who see it differently. Some of the clearest articulations of the tension come from inside the community building these solutions.
Jessica Janiuk, a Google engineer working on the Angular framework, spent six to eight months researching partial hydration across the ecosystem before presenting her findings at a web conference. Her conclusion, delivered plainly: partial hydration had become a trendy topic among framework authors, everyone was talking about it, and almost no one agreed on what it actually meant in practice. Despite the volume of discussion, she found that genuine partial hydration support across major frameworks was far rarer than the conversation around it would suggest.
Read that carefully. This is a senior engineer at one of the largest framework teams in the industry, after months of dedicated research, reporting that the industry has more marketing language around partial hydration than actual shared understanding or working implementations. That’s not a sign of a mature solution. That’s a sign of an industry-wide patch effort still figuring out what it’s even patching.
Ryan Carniato, creator of SolidJS, has been equally direct in interviews and writing about why partial hydration is hard: it requires components that don’t rerun unnecessarily, fine-grained update mechanisms, and architectural decisions made from the start of a framework’s design, not bolted on afterward. He’s noted that the approach only works well in certain types of applications, and that frameworks retrofitting it onto an existing full-hydration model face a much harder problem than one designed around partial hydration from day one.
That’s the whole argument in one sentence from the person who builds these systems for a living: partial hydration works when you design for it from the beginning. It’s a struggle when you’re trying to graft it onto an architecture that assumed full hydration was always the answer.
Why “Retrofit” Is the Right Word
Look at the terminology the industry has produced trying to solve this: islands, progressive hydration, donut hydration, selective hydration, resumability, incremental hydration. Janiuk’s research specifically flagged the sheer number of near-synonymous terms, each framework inventing its own vocabulary for a version of the same underlying fix.
When an industry generates that much terminology for the same corrective pattern, it’s not because the pattern is exotic or cutting-edge. It’s because everyone independently ran into the same wall, the cost of hydrating an entire page when most of it never needed to be interactive, and each framework built its own patch, in its own vocabulary, without ever fully agreeing on the fix.
Compare that to a page that never fully hydrates in the first place because it was never fully client-rendered to begin with. There’s no terminology needed for “the server rendered the HTML and the parts that need interactivity use a small script for it.” That’s not a pattern requiring a name and a conference talk. That’s the default behavior of the web platform before the SPA model decided interactivity had to be the framework’s job everywhere, all the time, whether the page needed it or not.
What Starting From the Correct Premise Looks Like
The alternative isn’t a cleverer hydration strategy. It’s not needing one.
A server-rendered page built with HTML fragments and a lightweight interaction layer like HTMX doesn’t hydrate anything, because there’s no client-side virtual representation of the page waiting to be reconciled with the real DOM. The server already decided what the HTML looks like. When something needs to change, the server sends the new HTML, and the browser swaps it in. There’s no “island” of interactivity requiring special hydration treatment, because the entire page was never wrapped in a hydration requirement to begin with.
This connects to a broader instinct in web development that predates the current framework era. Smashing Magazine’s coverage of brutalist web design captured a version of this same argument: a return to raw, unadorned, performance-first construction as a reaction against unnecessary complexity layered on top of simple content delivery. The instinct isn’t new. Static HTML with targeted interactivity was the web’s original architecture. Islands architecture is the SPA ecosystem rediscovering it, one hydration strategy at a time, and giving it a new name each time a different framework reaches the same conclusion independently.
The Actual Claim
I’m not arguing that islands architecture is bad engineering. Jason Miller’s framing was a genuine improvement over shipping a full SPA runtime for content that never needed it, and frameworks like Astro have built serious, well-designed systems around the idea. The engineering is good.
The claim is narrower and, I think, more useful: the need for that engineering in the first place is itself evidence. When your framework requires a dedicated architecture, a body of research, and industry-wide terminology debates just to selectively undo its own default behavior, the default was the mistake, not the workaround. Logical Brutalism, and the broader server-driven approach it’s part of, doesn’t need an islands strategy, because it never made static content pay the hydration tax it’s now trying to refund.
What does not resolve, does not exist.
